chunk_summary: This section discusses hypernatremia risk in children, its clinical signs, safe correction protocols, and maintenance intravenous fluid practices, emphasizing isotonic solutions and careful monitoring to prevent complications like cerebral edema.
learning_objectives: Recognize clinical signs and risk factors for hypernatremic dehydration in children.
Understand safe correction protocols for hypernatremia, including sodium reduction rates and fluid management.
Apply guidelines for maintenance intravenous fluid therapy using isotonic solutions in pediatric patients.
Identify high-risk situations requiring close electrolyte monitoring in children.
key_facts: Hypernatremia correction should reduce serum sodium by no more than 0.5 mmol/L per hour or 10–12 mmol/L per day.
Initial resuscitation for compromised circulation involves isotonic boluses, followed by gradual deficit replacement over 48 hours.
Maintenance IV fluids for children >28 days should use 0.9% saline-based solutions with dextrose; potassium is added only after urine output is established.
Maintenance fluid volume is calculated using the Holliday–Segar method: 4 mL/kg/hour for the first 10 kg, 2 mL/kg/hour for the next 10 kg, and 1 mL/kg/hour thereafter.
High-risk situations for electrolyte monitoring include pneumonia, bronchiolitis, meningitis, postoperative states, and ICU care.
Neonates and preterm infants require specialized protocols due to different renal physiology.
reference_quote: AAP and NICE guidance supports 0.9% saline-based maintenance with dextrose; add potassium only after urine output is established.
document_key_topics: Hypernatremia risk and clinical signs in children
Safe correction of hypernatremia and prevention of complications
Guidelines for maintenance intravenous fluid therapy
High-risk situations for electrolyte monitoring in pediatric care
